serving.natural_language_portion: One standard jin deui ball weighs approximately 60g and is the typical single-piece serving at dim sum or as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: Jin Deui / Sesame Ball — Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Jin Deui / Sesame Ball. HPB Foodo is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glutinous Rice and Glutinous Rice Flour Products — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jin deui as a composite dish. GI of 85 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice flour (cooked), which reports GI values in the range of 90–98. A composite GI of 85 is applied to account for partial GI suppression from fat (frying oil, sesame seeds) and dietary fibre from red bean paste and sesame, consistent with published methodology for composite fried glutinous rice products. Glycemic load per 60g serving = (85 × 27.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.2.

hypertension.guidance: Jin deui is a sweet dim sum item with very low sodium; a single 60g piece delivers only 51mg sodium, which is well within safe limits for hypertensive patients, though the high caloric density from frying should be considered in the context of overall dietary energy management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI 85) and high glycemic load per serving (GL 23.2) driven by glutinous rice flour shell and sweetened red bean paste filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid jin deui or limit intake to a maximum of half a ball (approximately 30g, GL ~11.6), as the glutinous rice flour base is one of the most glycaemically potent common food starches and the sweetened filling further elevates postprandial blood glucose; the sesame coating does not meaningfully reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (14.5g/100g) from deep-frying with low saturated fat (2.2g/100g); zero trans fat; sesame seeds contribute beneficial lignans and unsaturated fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Jin deui is deep-fried but has a relatively low saturated fat content (2.2g/100g) and confirmed zero trans fat; however, the high caloric density (325 kcal/100g) and high glycaemic load make regular consumption inadvisable for patients with cardiovascular disease who require weight and glycaemic management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GI from glutinous rice (proinflammatory via glycaemic mechanism) partially offset by anti-inflammatory sesamin lignans from sesame seeds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high glycaemic index of glutinous rice flour may promote systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, sesame seeds contribute sesamin (an anti-inflammatory lignan), selenium (12mcg/100g), and calcium (75mg/100g), providing partial offsetting benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jin Deui (煎堆), known in Mandarin as Zhī Má Qiú (芝麻球, sesame balls), are spherical deep-fried glutinous rice pastries that are a classic fixture of Cantonese dim sum and a traditional Chinese New Year food symbolising wealth and prosperity — the ball expands dramatically during frying, representing growing fortune. The outer shell is made from glutinous rice flour dough rolled in white sesame seeds; during deep-frying in vegetable oil, the shell puffs up to create a hollow, airy interior with a thin, crispy, sesame-studded crust. The filling is sweetened red bean paste (dou sha / 豆沙), which is soft, fragrant, and mildly sweet. Jin deui are widely available at dim sum restaurants, Chinese dessert stalls, and kopitiams across Singapore, Malaysia, and the Chinese diaspora. Nutritionally, jin deui provide 325 kcal per 100g, with 6.2g protein, 14.5g fat (predominantly unsaturated from sesame and frying oil), and 45.5g carbohydrate. The glutinous rice flour base has a very high glycemic index (GI ~85), making jin deui one of the most glycaemically potent items at dim sum. A standard 60g piece delivers a glycemic load of 23.2, which is clinically significant for patients with type 2 diabetes. Sesame seeds contribute beneficial sesamin lignans, calcium (75mg/100g), selenium (12mcg/100g), and iron (2.8mg/100g). Sodium is low at 85mg/100g. Jin deui are naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. The primary allergen is sesame.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous rice flour (cooked) with documented analogous-estimate methodology. All mandatory multilingual names are present.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for jin deui is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; these values are not available from the approved primary source. water_g set to -1: moisture content for jin deui is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source (plant-based ingredients only; no animal fat used). vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero as jin deui contains no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source. GI value of 85 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for glutinous rice flour (cooked, GI 90–98), adjusted downward to 85 to account for partial GI suppression from frying fat and dietary fibre from sesame seeds and red bean paste. Glycemic load per 60g serving calculated as (85 × 27.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.2.
